text: We present our experience in the laparoscopic treatment of an aneurysm of the splenic artery.
This was a fifty-five year old woman who was being evaluated for kidney stones and she was incidentally diagnosed with splenic artery aneurysm.
Her past medical history was significant for IBS and asthma as a child.

During her pre-op evaluation, a CT scan of the abdomen showed a round structure consistent with aneurysm of the splenic artery, with calcifications where the presence of blood flow could not be excluded.
The coronal view reveals the splenic artery aneurysm, visible as a round structure with calcifications.

Arterogram of the splenic artery demonstrated a stop image in its mid-portion and its continuation through collateral pancreatic circulation, redirecting the flow towards the distal portion of the splenic artery.
The surgeon uses a Nathanson liver retractor to elevate the liver, providing clear access to the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the part flaccida, exposing the retrocavity behind the stomach, revealing the anatomical space for further dissection.
A round structure, consistent with a splenic artery aneurysm, is visible on the superior edge of the pancreas.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the splenic aneurysm from the surrounding retroperitoneal tissue, carefully separating it from adjacent structures.
The surgeon continues dissection towards the pancreas using blunt maneuvers, carefully separating tissue layers around the pancreatic region.
The surgeon transects a collateral vessel originating from the aneurysm, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues dissection towards the retroperitoneum, progressing through the surgical field with visible tissue separation.
The surgeon dissects the splenic artery proximal to the aneurysm, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The splenic vein is visible in the surgical field, surrounded by dissected tissue.
The surgeon completes the dissection using a right-angle instrument, focusing on the area near the pancreas.
The surgeon places a metallic vascular clamp on a vessel, securing it during the splenectomy procedure.
The spleen is visually inspected, showing no signs of ischemia.
The surgeon uses a hook electrocautery device to dissect the distal portion of the splenic artery, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses Hemolog clips to secure the splenic artery, ensuring hemostasis during the procedure.
The surgeon transects the splenic artery using scissors, completing the dissection process.
The surgeon transects the collateral vessels using a harmonic scalpel, visibly cutting through the tissue with precision.
The surgeon exposes the aneurysm by retracting the distal portion of the splenic artery, revealing the structure within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel and blunt dissection to continue freeing the aneurysm from the surrounding pancreatic tissue.
The surgeon clips all visible vessels in the surgical field, ensuring hemostasis before proceeding with the splenectomy.
The surgeon completes the dissection, leaving the proximal portion of the splenic artery visibly exposed.
The surgeon applies Hemolog clips to secure the exposed proximal portion of the splenic artery, ensuring hemostasis following the dissection and transection of collateral vessels.
The surgeon retrieves the vascular clamp from the surgical field, following the exposure and manipulation of the splenic artery and surrounding structures.
The surgeon transects the proximal portion of the splenic artery using scissors, following the exposure and dissection of the artery from surrounding tissue.
The spleen remains unchanged in appearance, with no visible alterations following the previous surgical steps.
The surgeon places the spleen specimen into an endobag and retrieves it through one of the trochanter incisions.
patient recovered without complications and she was discharged home on post-op day number three.
